      fx:(x,y)=> -3*x*x - 2*x,
      fy:(x,y)=> 2*y,
      label:'y² − x³ − x²',
      singAt: [[0,0]],
      xr:[-1.5,2.5], yr:[-2,2]
    },
    'y2-x3': {
      f: (x,y)=> y*y - x*x*x,
      fx:(x,y)=> -3*x*x,
      fy:(x,y)=> 2*y,
      label:'y² − x³',
      singAt: [[0,0]],
      xr:[-0.5,2.5], yr:[-2.5,2.5]
    },
    'xy': {
      f: (x,y)=> x*y,
      fx:(x,y)=> y,
      fy:(x,y)=> x,
      label:'xy',
      singAt:[[0,0]],
      xr:[-2,2], yr:[-2,2]
    },
    'y2-x3-1': {
      f: (x,y)=> y*y - x*x*x - 1,
      fx:(x,y)=> -3*x*x,
      fy:(x,y)=> 2*y,
      label:'y² − x³ − 1',
      singAt:[],
      xr:[-1.5,2.5], yr:[-3,3]
    },
    'x2+y2-1': {
      f:(x,y)=> x*x+y*y-1,
      fx:(x,y)=> 2*x,
      fy:(x,y)=> 2*y,
      label:'x² + y² − 1',
      singAt:[],
      xr:[-1.6,1.6], yr:[-1.6,1.6]
    },
    'x2-y2': {
      f:(x,y)=> x*x-y*y,
      fx:(x,y)=> 2*x,
      fy:(x,y)=> -2*y,
      label:'x² − y²',
      singAt:[[0,0]],
      xr:[-2,2], yr:[-2,2]
    }
  };

  const pick = $('#tan-pick'), aIn = $('#tan-a'), bIn = $('#tan-b'),
        go = $('#tan-go'), out = $('#tan-out'), svg = $('#tan-svg'),
        verdict = $('#tan-verdict');

  function draw(curve, a, b){
    svg.innerHTML = '';
    const S = 100; // scale for ±1 math unit -> 100 px
    const tx = x => x*S;
    const ty = y => -y*S;
    // axes
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:-130,y1:0,x2:130,y2:0,stroke:'var(--line)','stroke-width':1}));
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:0,y1:-130,x2:0,y2:130,stroke:'var(--line)','stroke-width':1}));
    // plot curve via marching squares (implicit curve)
    const N = 120;
    const xmin=-1.5, xmax=1.5, ymin=-1.5, ymax=1.5;
    const step = 3/N;
    for(let i=0;i<N;i++) for(let j=0;j<N;j++){
      const x0=xmin+i*step, x1=xmin+(i+1)*step;
      const y0=ymin+j*step, y1=ymin+(j+1)*step;
      const v00=curve.f(x0,y0), v10=curve.f(x1,y0),
            v11=curve.f(x1,y1), v01=curve.f(x0,y1);
      // draw edges where sign changes
      function isect(va,vb,pa,pb){
        const t = va/(va-vb);
        return [pa[0]+t*(pb[0]-pa[0]), pa[1]+t*(pb[1]-pa[1])];
      }
      const pts = [];
      if((v00>0)!==(v10>0)) pts.push(isect(v00,v10,[x0,y0],[x1,y0]));
      if((v10>0)!==(v11>0)) pts.push(isect(v10,v11,[x1,y0],[x1,y1]));
      if((v11>0)!==(v01>0)) pts.push(isect(v11,v01,[x1,y1],[x0,y1]));
      if((v01>0)!==(v00>0)) pts.push(isect(v01,v00,[x0,y1],[x0,y0]));
      if(pts.length===2){
        svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{
          x1:tx(pts[0][0]), y1:ty(pts[0][1]),
          x2:tx(pts[1][0]), y2:ty(pts[1][1]),
          stroke:'var(--blue)','stroke-width':1.4
        }));
      }
    }
    // point
    if(isFinite(a) && isFinite(b)){
      const onCurve = Math.abs(curve.f(a,b)) < 1e-6;
      const fxv = curve.fx(a,b), fyv = curve.fy(a,b);
      const sing = onCurve && Math.abs(fxv)<1e-9 && Math.abs(fyv)<1e-9;
      // draw tangent line if smooth & on curve
      if(onCurve && !sing){
        // tangent direction: perpendicular to (fx,fy) is (fy,-fx)
        const dx = fyv, dy = -fxv;
        const L = Math.hypot(dx,dy);
        const ux = dx/L, uy = dy/L;
        const TL = 1.2;
        svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{
          x1: tx(a-TL*ux), y1: ty(b-TL*uy),
          x2: tx(a+TL*ux), y2: ty(b+TL*uy),
          stroke:'var(--yellow)','stroke-width':1.6, 'stroke-dasharray':'4 3'
        }));
      }
      const col = !onCurve ? 'var(--mute)' : (sing ? 'var(--pink)' : 'var(--green)');
      svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:tx(a),cy:ty(b),r:5,fill:col,stroke:'#111','stroke-width':1.5}));
    }
  }

  function compute(){
    const key = pick.value;
    const curve = CURVES[key];
    const a = parseFloat(aIn.value), b = parseFloat(bIn.value);
    const lines = [];
    lines.push(`f = ${curve.label}`);
    lines.push(`p = (${a}, ${b})`);
    const fval = curve.f(a,b);
    const onCurve = Math.abs(fval) < 1e-6;
    lines.push(`f(p) = ${fval.toFixed(3)}`);
    if(!onCurve){
      lines.push('point is not on V(f) — pick (a,b) satisfying f=0');
      verdict.textContent = 'off the curve';
      verdict.style.color = 'var(--mute)';
      draw(curve,a,b);
      out.textContent = lines.join('\n');
      return;
    }
    const fxv = curve.fx(a,b), fyv = curve.fy(a,b);
    lines.push(`∇f(p) = (${fxv.toFixed(3)}, ${fyv.toFixed(3)})`);
    const sing = Math.abs(fxv)<1e-9 && Math.abs(fyv)<1e-9;
    if(sing){
      lines.push('Jacobian is zero at p — T_p V(f) = k², dim = 2');
      lines.push('but dim V(f) = 1, so p is SINGULAR');
      lines.push('(the local ring 𝒪_p is not regular: dim 𝔪/𝔪² = 2 > 1 = dim)');
      verdict.textContent = 'singular';
      verdict.style.color = 'var(--pink)';
    } else {
      lines.push(`ker(∇f|_p) = line through (${fyv.toFixed(2)}, ${(-fxv).toFixed(2)})`);
      lines.push('T_p V(f) = 1-dimensional');
      lines.push('dim V(f) = 1 = dim T_p → p is SMOOTH');
      lines.push(`(local ring 𝒪_p is a regular local ring of dim 1 — a DVR)`);
      verdict.textContent = 'smooth';
      verdict.style.color = 'var(--green)';
    }
    draw(curve,a,b);
    out.textContent = lines.join('\n');
  }

  go.addEventListener('click', compute);
  pick.addEventListener('change',()=>{
    // suggest a natural point
    const c = CURVES[pick.value];
    if(c.singAt.length){ aIn.value = c.singAt[0][0]; bIn.value = c.singAt[0][1]; }
    compute();
  });
  aIn.addEventListener('input', compute);
  bIn.addEventListener('input', compute);
  compute();
})();
</script>

blocks.html: The proof of Nakayama is a one-line determinant trick: if $m_1,\dots,m_n$ generate $M$ and $\mathfrak{m}M=M$, write each $m_i=\sum_j a_{ij}m_j$ with $a_{ij}\in\mathfrak{m}$; Cayley–Hamilton applied to the matrix $a=(a_{ij})$ yields $\det(I-a)\cdot m_i = 0$ for all $i$. Since $\det(I-a)\equiv 1\pmod{\mathfrak{m}}$ it is a unit, forcing $M=0$. The same trick gives the determinantal form: if $\phi\colon M\to M$ satisfies $\phi(M)\subset\mathfrak{a}M$ for an ideal $\mathfrak{a}$, then $\phi$ satisfies a monic polynomial with non-leading coefficients in $\mathfrak{a}$.

blocks.html:
11. Integral extensions and Noether normalization

Fix a ring extension $A\subseteq B$. An element $b\in B$ is integral over $A$ if it satisfies a monic polynomial

$b^n + a_{n-1}b^{n-1} + \cdots + a_1 b + a_0 = 0,\qquad a_i\in A.$

Equivalently, $A[b]\subseteq B$ is a finitely generated $A$-module. The set of integral elements is a subring — the integral closure of $A$ in $B$ — and $B$ is an integral extension when every element is integral. This generalizes "algebraic number" from $\mathbb{Z}\subseteq\overline{\mathbb{Q}}$ to arbitrary rings; $\mathcal{O}_K$ is the integral closure of $\mathbb{Z}$ in the number field $K$.

Three foundational theorems.
  • Lying over. For $A\subseteq B$ integral and $\mathfrak{p}\subset A$ prime, there exists a prime $\mathfrak{q}\subset B$ with $\mathfrak{q}\cap A=\mathfrak{p}$; the induced map $\mathrm{Spec}(B)\to\mathrm{Spec}(A)$ is surjective.
  • Going up. A chain $\mathfrak{p}_0\subsetneq\mathfrak{p}_1\subsetneq\cdots$ in $A$ lifts to a chain $\mathfrak{q}_0\subsetneq\mathfrak{q}_1\subsetneq\cdots$ in $B$ above it; hence $\dim A=\dim B$ for integral extensions.
  • Incomparability. If $\mathfrak{q}\subsetneq\mathfrak{q}'$ are primes of $B$ with $\mathfrak{q}\cap A=\mathfrak{q}'\cap A$, then $\mathfrak{q}=\mathfrak{q}'$ — fibers are zero-dimensional.

Noether normalization. If $B$ is a finitely generated $k$-algebra of Krull dimension $d$, there exist algebraically independent $y_1,\dots,y_d\in B$ with $k[y_1,\dots,y_d]\hookrightarrow B$ a finite (in particular integral) extension. Geometrically: every affine variety admits a finite surjection to some $\mathbb{A}^d_k$, projecting it onto a polynomial coordinate system. This is the engine behind Hilbert's Nullstellensatz: maximal ideals of $k[x_1,\dots,x_n]$ with $k=\overline k$ are exactly $(x_1-a_1,\dots,x_n-a_n)$, proved by showing any residue field $k[x_1,\dots,x_n]/\mathfrak{m}$ is finite over $k$ via normalization, hence equals $k$.

Concretely: for $B=k[x,y]/(y^2-x^3-x)$ one can take $y_1=x$; then $B$ is a degree-2 extension of $k[x]$, realized by the covering map $(x,y)\mapsto x$ that exhibits the elliptic curve as a double cover of the line branched at the roots of $x^3+x$. Going-up is why this projection is surjective on points.

Integrality checker

params.bodyScript:
<script>
/* =============================== SECTION 10: integrality checker =============================== */
(function(){
  const DATA = {
    'sqrt-5': {
      label:'α = √−5',
      monic:'t² + 5 = 0',
      integral:true,
      reason:'α² = −5 ∈ ℤ, so α satisfies t² + 5 = 0 (monic, coefficients in ℤ).',
      ring:'α generates the ring of integers ℤ[√−5] ⊂ ℚ(√−5).'
    },
    'cbrt2': {
      label:'α = ∛2',
      monic:'t³ − 2 = 0',
      integral:true,
      reason:'α³ = 2, so α satisfies t³ − 2 = 0 (monic).',
      ring:'ℤ[∛2] is the ring of integers of ℚ(∛2).'
    },
    'half': {
      label:'α = 1/2',
      monic:'no monic integer polynomial vanishes at 1/2',
      integral:false,
      reason:'Rational roots of a monic ℤ[t] polynomial must divide the constant term and be integers (rational root theorem) — so the only rational integral elements over ℤ are ordinary integers.',
      ring:'1/2 is algebraic (satisfies 2t − 1) but not integral.'
    },
    'gaussian': {
      label:'α = i',
      monic:'t² + 1 = 0',
      integral:true,
      reason:'i² = −1, so i satisfies t² + 1 = 0.',
      ring:'ℤ[i] = Gaussian integers, the integral closure of ℤ in ℚ(i).'
    },
    'third': {
      label:'α = 1/3',
      monic:'no monic integer polynomial vanishes at 1/3',
      integral:false,
      reason:'Same reason as 1/2: rational root theorem blocks non-integer rationals.',
      ring:'1/3 satisfies 3t − 1 = 0 — non-monic, so not integral.'
    },
    'phi': {
      label:'α = (1+√5)/2',
      monic:'t² − t − 1 = 0',
      integral:true,
      reason:'Direct check: ((1+√5)/2)² = (6+2√5)/4 = (3+√5)/2 = 1 + (1+√5)/2. So α² = α + 1.',
      ring:'ℤ[α] is the ring of integers of ℚ(√5) — the "full" ring, not just ℤ[√5].'
    },
    'rootof': {
      label:'α = y where y² = x³ + x',
      monic:'t² − (x³+x) = 0 ∈ k[x][t]',
      integral:true,
      reason:'y satisfies t² − (x³+x) = 0, monic over k[x].',
      ring:'k[x,y]/(y²−x³−x) is a degree-2 integral extension of k[x] — the affine elliptic curve, covered 2:1 by projection to the x-line.'
    }
  };
  const pick = $('#int-ring'), out = $('#int-out');
  function draw(){
    const D = DATA[pick.value];
    let msg = `${D.label}\n\nmonic relation: ${D.monic}\n\nreason: ${D.reason}\n\n`;
    msg += D.integral ? '→ INTEGRAL ✓' : '→ NOT integral ✗';
    msg += `\n${D.ring}`;
    out.textContent = msg;
  }
  pick.addEventListener('change', draw);
  draw();
})();
</script>

      reason:'y satisfies t² − (x³+x) = 0, monic over k[x].',
      ring:'k[x,y]/(y²−x³−x) is a degree-2 integral extension of k[x] — the affine elliptic curve, covered 2:1 by projection to the x-line.'
    }
  };
  const pick = $('#int-ring'), out = $('#int-out');
  function draw(){
    const D = DATA[pick.value];
    let msg = `${D.label}\n\nmonic relation: ${D.monic}\n\nreason: ${D.reason}\n\n`;
    msg += D.integral ? '→ INTEGRAL ✓' : '→ NOT integral ✗';
    msg += `\n${D.ring}`;
    out.textContent = msg;
  }
  pick.addEventListener('change', draw);

})();

blocks.html:
12. Dimension and primary decomposition

The Krull dimension of a ring $A$ is the supremum of lengths $n$ of chains

$\mathfrak{p}_0\subsetneq\mathfrak{p}_1\subsetneq\cdots\subsetneq\mathfrak{p}_n$

of prime ideals. It's the algebraic shadow of "dimension of a variety". The basic table:

$\dim \mathbb{Z} = 1$ (chains $(0)\subsetneq(p)$) · $\dim k = 0$ · $\dim k[x] = 1$ · $\dim k[x_1,\dots,x_n] = n$ · $\dim \mathbb{Z}[x] = 2$. In general $\dim A[x]=\dim A + 1$ for $A$ Noetherian.

For quotients: $\dim A/I$ = longest chain of primes of $A$ containing $I$. Example: $k[x,y]/(xy)$ has $\dim = 1$: its Spec is two coordinate lines meeting at the origin; the longest chain is (generic point of one line) $\subsetneq$ (origin).

Primary decomposition generalizes the fundamental theorem of arithmetic. Every ideal in a Noetherian ring admits $I=\bigcap_i Q_i$ with each $Q_i$ primary; the associated primes $\sqrt{Q_i}$ are intrinsic. For $(n)\subset\mathbb{Z}$ this is just $n=\prod p_i^{e_i}$.

    // group by height
    const heights = [...new Set(R.primes.map(p=>p.height))].sort((a,b)=>a-b);
    const maxH = Math.max(...heights, 0);
    const layers = {};
    for(const p of R.primes) (layers[p.height]=layers[p.height]||[]).push(p);
    const pos = {};
    const padT=50, padB=30;
    const yStep = maxH===0 ? 0 : (H-padT-padB)/maxH;
    for(const h of heights){
      const layer = layers[h];
      layer.forEach((p,i)=>{
        const x = W*(i+1)/(layer.length+1);
        const y = padT + h*yStep;
        pos[p.id] = [x,y];
      });

      const [x1,y1]=pos[lo], [x2,y2]=pos[up];

      let col = 'var(--blue)';
      if(p.height===0) col = 'var(--violet)';
      else if(p.height===maxH) col = 'var(--pink)';
      else col = 'var(--yellow)';
      const g = SVG('g'); g.style.cursor='pointer';
      const isSel = selectedId===p.id;
      const c = SVG('circle',{cx:x,cy:y,r:isSel?size+3:size, fill:col, stroke:isSel?'#fff':'#111','stroke-width':isSel?2:1});

      lab.textContent = p.label;
      g.appendChild(c); g.appendChild(lab);
      g.addEventListener('click',()=>{ selectedId=p.id; render(); });
      svg.appendChild(g);
    }
    // legend: height bands
    for(const h of heights){
      const ytxt = padT + h*yStep;
      const band = SVG('text',{x:16,y:ytxt+4,'font-size':11,fill:'var(--mute)'});
      band.textContent = `h=${h}`;
      svg.appendChild(band);
    }
    // info
    const sel = R.primes.find(p=>p.id===selectedId) || R.primes[0];
    let info = `ring: ${R.title}\nKrull dim = ${R.dim}\n\n`;
    info += `selected: ${sel.label}\n  height = ${sel.height}\n  ${sel.desc}\n\n`;
    // show longest chain
    info += 'longest chains:\n';
    function chainsFrom(id,path){
      const outs = R.edges.filter(([a])=>a===id).map(([_,b])=>b);
      if(outs.length===0) return [path];
      const all = [];
      for(const o of outs) all.push(...chainsFrom(o, path.concat(o)));
      return all;
    }
    const mins = R.primes.filter(p=>p.height===0).map(p=>p.id);
    let chains = [];
    for(const m of mins) chains.push(...chainsFrom(m,[m]));
    if(chains.length===0 && mins.length){
      chains = mins.map(m=>[m]);
    }
    chains.sort((a,b)=>b.length-a.length);
    const top = chains.slice(0, Math.min(3, chains.length));
    for(const ch of top){
      info += '  ' + ch.map(id=>R.primes.find(p=>p.id===id).label).join(' ⊂ ') + '\n';
    }
    out.textContent = info;
  }
  pick.addEventListener('change',()=>{selectedId=null; render();});
  render();
})();
</script>

blocks.html:
Notice how $k[x,y]/(xy)$ (union of axes) has dimension $1$ even though it sits inside the $2$-dimensional affine plane: Krull dimension sees the biggest component. The height of a prime is its length as a chain ending there; the coheight (dimension of the closure) complements it to $\dim A$ in nice cases (catenary rings).

Primary decomposition preview

For $I=(xy)\subset k[x,y]$ the decomposition is $I=(x)\cap(y)$, recovering the two irreducible components. For $I=(x^2, xy)=(x)\cap(x,y)^2$ in $k[x,y]$ — two components, one embedded in the other — primary decomposition is how scheme-theoretic multiplicity makes its appearance.

blocks.html:
13. Transcendence degree and geometric dimension

For a field extension $K/k$, the transcendence degree $\mathrm{tr.deg}_k(K)$ is the cardinality of a maximal algebraically independent subset of $K$ over $k$ — the "number of free parameters" separating $K$ from $k$. All such subsets have the same size (a Steinitz-style exchange argument). A transcendence basis extends to $K$ via an algebraic extension.

The bridge theorem. If $B$ is a finitely generated integral domain over a field $k$, then $$\dim B = \mathrm{tr.deg}_k\,\mathrm{Frac}(B).$$ Proof: apply Noether normalization to find $k[y_1,\dots,y_d]\hookrightarrow B$ finite with $d=\mathrm{tr.deg}$; integral extensions preserve dimension; $\dim k[y_1,\dots,y_d]=d$.

    'kxy': { title:'k[x,y]', dim:2, trdeg:2, basis:'{x, y}', desc:'affine plane — two free parameters' },

blocks.html:
Two corollaries worth remembering:

dim of intersection If $V, W\subseteq\mathbb{A}^n$ are irreducible varieties of dimensions $d_1, d_2$, each irreducible component of $V\cap W$ has dimension $\ge d_1+d_2-n$. Equality is "proper intersection"; it fails for tangent components (excess intersection).

dim of fibre For a dominant morphism $f\colon X\to Y$ of irreducible varieties, the generic fibre has dimension $\dim X - \dim Y$; special fibres can only jump up (upper-semicontinuity).

Transcendence degree also controls the function-field side of the dictionary: birational equivalence of varieties $\Leftrightarrow$ isomorphism of their fraction fields over $k$. So birational classification of curves ($\mathrm{tr.deg}=1$) is the classification of one-variable function fields, which Riemann-Roch organizes by genus.

    let msg = `ring: ${R.title}\n${R.desc}\n\n`;
    msg += `Krull dim = ${R.dim}\n`;
    msg += `tr.deg_k(Frac(·)) = ${R.trdeg}\n`;
    msg += `transcendence basis: ${R.basis}\n\n`;
    msg += (R.dim === R.trdeg)
      ? '✓ dim = tr.deg (bridge theorem holds)'
      : '⚠ mismatch (should not happen for f.g. domains)';
    out.textContent = msg;
  }
  pick.addEventListener('change', draw);
  draw();
})();
</script>

The pattern running through these twelve sections: every classical operation (taking a subvariety, zooming in, linearizing, perturbing, counting parameters) has an exact algebraic shadow — quotient, localization, tangent space via $\mathfrak{m}/\mathfrak{m}^2$, flatness, transcendence degree. Commutative algebra is the language that lets you turn geometric questions into finite computations on ideals and modules, and vice versa. The payoff, once you start using schemes seriously, is that the two pictures collapse into one.
